entry.title: What 20+ Years of Working with SAP Has Taught Me About Successful Transformations
entry.content:
What 20+ Years of Working with SAP Has Taught Me About Successful Transformations

Introduction

Over the past two decades, I have had the opportunity to work on SAP programs across multiple industries, including manufacturing, distribution, consumer products, and food services. During this journey, I have participated in SAP implementations, upgrades, global rollouts, business process transformations, and large-scale SAP S/4HANA migration programs.

Technology has changed significantly during this time. We have moved from traditional ERP implementations to intelligent enterprise platforms powered by real-time analytics, cloud technologies, automation, and artificial intelligence. However, despite these advancements, I have noticed that the factors that determine transformation success remain surprisingly consistent.

This article shares some of the most important lessons I have learned from working on SAP programs over the years.

Technology Alone Does Not Guarantee Success

Early in my career, I believed that selecting the right technology would automatically lead to project success. Over time, I learned that technology is only one part of the equation.

I have seen technically sound implementations struggle because business users were not prepared, process changes were not fully understood, or organizational alignment was missing.

The most successful SAP programs focus equally on people, processes, governance, and technology.

Organizations that treat SAP transformations as business transformations rather than IT projects typically achieve better outcomes.

Data Quality Matters More Than Most Organizations Expect

One recurring challenge I have observed across SAP programs is data quality.

Many organizations underestimate the effort required to cleanse, standardize, and validate data before migration activities begin.

In several transformation programs, data issues became one of the largest sources of testing delays, reconciliation challenges, and post-go-live support incidents.

My experience has taught me that data preparation should begin early and remain a continuous focus throughout the transformation lifecycle.

The quality of the migrated data often determines how quickly organizations realize value after deployment.

Governance Should Be Proactive, Not Reactive

Another lesson I have learned is that governance cannot be treated as a reporting exercise.

Many projects rely heavily on status updates and issue tracking. While these activities are important, effective governance requires early identification of risks and proactive decision-making.

The strongest transformation programs establish clear escalation paths, executive involvement, readiness monitoring, and structured decision-making processes from the beginning.

When governance becomes reactive, issues often grow larger before corrective actions are taken.

Business Engagement Is Critical

SAP transformations affect how people perform their daily work.

Because of this, business participation cannot be limited to requirements gathering and testing activities.

Successful programs consistently involve business stakeholders throughout the project lifecycle.

This includes:

  • Process design reviews

  • Data validation activities

  • Testing participation

  • Readiness assessments

  • Change management initiatives

The more engaged the business becomes, the smoother the transition tends to be after go-live.

Testing Is About Confidence, Not Just Defect Detection

Many organizations view testing primarily as a mechanism for identifying defects.

While defect identification is important, testing serves a broader purpose.

Testing builds confidence.

It helps project teams, business users, and leadership understand whether the organization is truly prepared for deployment.

When testing is approached as a business validation exercise rather than a technical checkpoint, the quality of outcomes often improves significantly.

The Importance of Executive Sponsorship

Throughout my career, I have observed a direct relationship between executive engagement and project success.

Executive sponsors help:

  • Remove organizational barriers

  • Resolve cross-functional conflicts

  • Secure resources

  • Accelerate decision-making

  • Reinforce transformation objectives

Programs with strong executive sponsorship typically maintain better momentum and experience fewer delays.

Go-Live Is Not the Finish Line

One of the biggest misconceptions in SAP programs is that success is measured by reaching go-live.

In reality, go-live represents the beginning of a new operational phase.

The true measure of success includes:

  • User adoption

  • Business process stability

  • Operational performance

  • Issue resolution effectiveness

  • Achievement of business objectives

Organizations that invest in hypercare, support readiness, and continuous improvement often realize greater long-term value from their SAP investments.

The Growing Role of AI in SAP Transformations

Recent advancements in artificial intelligence are creating new opportunities for SAP programs.

AI is increasingly being used to support:

  • Data quality monitoring

  • Predictive analytics

  • Risk identification

  • Testing optimization

  • Project governance

  • Operational insights

While AI is not a replacement for experience and judgment, it can significantly improve visibility and decision-making when applied appropriately.

I believe AI will continue to play an increasingly important role in future SAP transformation programs.

Final Thoughts

After more than 20 years of working with SAP, one lesson stands out above all others.

Successful transformations are rarely driven by technology alone.

They succeed when organizations align people, processes, data, governance, leadership, and technology toward a common objective.

Every SAP transformation presents unique challenges, but the principles of preparation, collaboration, business engagement, and continuous improvement remain constant.

As SAP technologies continue to evolve, these fundamentals will remain just as important as the platforms themselves.

From my experience, organizations that focus on these fundamentals consistently position themselves for stronger transformation outcomes and long-term business success.

entry.title: Improving SAP HANA DB Performance: Two Common CDS View Pitfalls and How to Fix Them
entry.content:
As application landscapes grow and user adoption increases, performance issues in custom CDS (Core Data Services) views can emerge in ways that are not immediately obvious.
Two patterns in particular are responsible for a significant share of unexpected CPU spikes in production HANA systems:

  1. CDS views using locale-based text retrieval that silently bypass HANA's most efficient execution engine.
  2. ABAP programs passing large filter lists to CDS views, generating thousands of SQL filter conditions at runtime.

Both patterns are common in custom development, both are easy to miss during development and testing, and both have clean, well-documented solutions. This post walks through each one in detail.

The technical foundation of this post is based on knowledge shared by @jayaprakash_subramanian .
His willingness to share his expertise on HANA execution internals and CDS optimization made this post possible - thank you.

Contents

  1. Step 0 — Diagnose Before You Fix
  2. Fix #1 — CDS Views with Locale Parameters Bypassing the HEX Engine
  3. Fix #2 — Large IN-List Conditions from ABAP Range Tables
  4. General Best Practices
  5. Summary
  6. References

Step 0: Before You Fix — Diagnose First

Before applying any optimization, confirm which queries are causing elevated CPU usage.
The SQL Plan Cache in SAP HANA is the primary tool for this.

Check the Execution Engine of a Specific Query

SELECT EXECUTION_ENGINE, STATEMENT_HASH, STATEMENT_STRING
FROM M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE
WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

The EXECUTION_ENGINE column reveals which engine processed the query:

ValueMeaning
HEXHigh-Performance Analytical Engine — optimal for CDS views
COLUMNColumn Engine — less optimized, higher CPU for CDS workloads
ROWRow Engine — used for row-store tables, not typical for CDS views

If you see COLUMN where you expect HEX, the query is not running at its full potential.

Monitor CPU and Memory at the System Level

SAP HANA Cockpit and HANA Studio both provide dashboards for CPU and memory usage over configurable time windows.
Key indicators to watch:

  • CPU usage spikes above 70–80% during business hours, correlated with Fiori app usage.
  • Out-of-Memory (OOM) dumps in HANA trace files or HANA Cockpit - these indicate memory pressure from specific statements.
  • Statement Memory Limit - if individual SQL statements approach the configured limit, they may be terminated or cause cascading failures.
Tip: Always capture both a 7-day and a 24-hour view to distinguish between sustained load and isolated spikes.
This distinction determines whether a fix is urgent or can be planned during a maintenance window.

CDS Views with Locale Parameters Bypassing the HEX Engine

SAP Note 3513507  ·  SAP Note 3076534

Background: How SAP HANA Handles Multilingual Text

SAP systems are designed to operate in multiple languages.
Many business entities - such as product descriptions, unit of measure texts, and customer category labels - are stored in dedicated text tables: separate database tables containing one row per language per entity.


Entity KeyLanguage KeyDescription
ITEM-001ENSteel Pipe
ITEM-001DEStahlrohr
ITEM-001HEצינור פלדה
ITEM-001JA鋼管

When a user opens a Fiori application with their session language set to Hebrew (he), the system must return the Hebrew text row and render it correctly in right-to-left script.
The same mechanism applies to any language that differs from the system installation default.

What WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) Does

In SAP HANA CDS, text tables are modeled using the @ObjectModel.text.association annotation.
When a Fiori application reads a CDS view backed by such an association, it automatically appends a WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) clause to the generated SQL query, passing the current user's session locale as the parameter value.

SELECT *
FROM MY_CDS_VIEW
WITH PARAMETERS ('LOCALE' = 'he')

This instructs HANA to join the text table and return only the rows matching the Hebrew language key.
Without this parameter, the CDS view would either return texts for all languages or fall back to a system default, neither of which is appropriate for a user-facing Fiori application.

Example — Hebrew locale in action

A product or entity search CDS view with LOCALE = 'he' returns Hebrew descriptions:

KeyDescription
ITEM-001צינור פלדה (Steel Pipe)
ITEM-002ספק ראשי בע"מ (Main Supplier Ltd.)
ITEM-003חברת דוגמה בע"מ (Example Company Ltd.)

The Problem: HEX Engine Limitation

SAP HANA's HEX (High-Performance Analytical Execution) Engine is the modern columnar execution engine optimized for CDS views accessed from Fiori applications.
It delivers substantially lower CPU consumption compared to the fallback Column Engine, particularly for queries involving joins, projections, and filters typical of CDS-based reads.

However, as documented in SAP Note 3513507, the HEX engine has a known limitation: it cannot process queries containing a WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) clause.
When such a query arrives, HANA silently falls back to the Column Engine.

Why this goes undetected: This fallback is entirely invisible during development and unit testing.
Results are correct - just processed less efficiently.
The impact only becomes apparent in production, where many users simultaneously access the same Fiori apps in a non-English locale.

This is particularly relevant in any deployment where the working language differs from the system installation default - including Hebrew, Arabic, Japanese, Chinese, Thai, and others.

The Solution: Adding the HEX Hint

There are two approaches, and the correct choice depends on whether you own the CDS view or not.
 
Prerequisite — must be applied before either option below
By default, SAP HANA excludes queries containing `WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?)` from HEX engine execution. Setting 'hex_enable_with_parameters_locale = true' in 'indexserver.ini' removes this restriction at the system level - without it, any hint applied at the view or statement level will have no effect on LOCALE queries.

Requires SAP HANA Revision >= 075 (SPS07).
Do not apply on SAP HANA <= 2.00.074 — see SAP Note 3513507.
ALTER SYSTEM ALTER CONFIGURATION ('indexserver.ini', 'SYSTEM')
SET ('sql','hex_enable_with_parameters_locale') = 'true'
WITH RECONFIGURE COMMENT 'SAP Note 3513507';

ALTER SYSTEM CLEAR SQL PLAN CACHE;

Option 1 — Preferred: Add the Hint Directly in the CDS View

If the CDS view is a custom view that you own and can modify, the preferred approach is to embed the DB hint directly in the CDS view definition using a consumption annotation. This is the clean, transport-safe, source-controlled solution described in SAP Note 2717265.
In the CDS view DDL, add the hint annotation:
@DatabaseHint: [{dbSystem: #HDB, hint: 'USE_HEX_PLAN'}]
define view entity YOUR_CDS_VIEW ...​
Or in the ABAP SELECT consuming the view, use the %_HINTS addition:
SELECT *
  FROM your_cds_view
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_results)
  %_HINTS HDB 'USE_HEX_PLAN'.
This approach keeps the optimization explicit, versioned, and visible in the development layer.

Option 2 — Fallback: ANNOTATION Hint at the HANA Layer

If the CDS view is a standard SAP-delivered view that you cannot modify, or if you need a temporary fix while a proper code change is being prepared, you can apply the hint at the HANA database layer using the ANNOTATION mechanism (SAP Note 3076534).
 
Important: This should not be the first choice. It is a workaround - applied at the database layer outside the ABAP development lifecycle.
Use it only when Option 1 is not applicable.
 
-- Apply the hint
ANNOTATE "YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME" SET 'HINT' = 'USE_HEX_PLAN';

-- Confirm the annotation is registered
SELECT * FROM ANNOTATIONS;

-- Clear the SQL Plan Cache so HANA generates a fresh execution plan
ALTER SYSTEM REMOVE SQL PLAN CACHE
  WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

To roll back:

ANNOTATE "YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME" UNSET 'HINT';

Regardless of which option is used, verify the result the same way:

SELECT EXECUTION_ENGINE, STATEMENT_HASH
FROM M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE
WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

The EXECUTION_ENGINE column should now show HEX.

Large IN-List Conditions from ABAP Range Tables

SAP Note 2399993 -  SAP HANA Fast Data Access (FDA)

The Problem: How ABAP Generates Thousands of SQL Filter Conditions

A common ABAP pattern for reading data from a CDS view is to build a range table and pass it using the IN operator:

SELECT *
FROM zcds_my_view
INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_results)
WHERE key_field IN @LR_range.

This is clean, readable ABAP - but it has a critical hidden cost at the database layer.
When ABAP's DB interface translates this statement into SQL, it expands the range table into a series of explicit filter conditions:

SELECT * FROM ZCDS_MY_VIEW
WHERE KEY_FIELD = 'VALUE001'
   OR KEY_FIELD = 'VALUE002'
   OR KEY_FIELD = 'VALUE003'
   -- ... potentially thousands more conditions

If lr_range contains hundreds or thousands of entries - entirely realistic for document processing, purchase order workflows, or batch jobs - HANA must parse and compile this statement each time it is executed.
This compilation overhead, not the data retrieval itself, is what drives CPU spikes and degraded response times.

Why this goes undetected: In development and testing, range tables are typically small (a handful of test records).
The problem scales silently with production data volumes, only surfacing once the system is under real load.

This pattern is additionally harmful because:

  • It generates near-identical but technically distinct SQL statements (different values each run), preventing effective plan cache reuse.
  • It worsens over time as data volumes and document counts grow.

The Solution: FOR ALL ENTRIES with HANA FDA Hint

Note: FDA for FOR ALL ENTRIES is enabled by default on ABAP kernel >= 7.45 (dbs/hdb/prefer_join_with_fda = 1).
The hint &prefer_join_with_fda 1& in the examples below is a per-statement control - useful for targeted application or troubleshooting - not a feature activation.
If you are on a modern kernel, the mechanism is already active system-wide.

Replace the IN operator with FOR ALL ENTRIES IN, combined with a HANA Fast Data Access (FDA) hint:

SELECT *
  FROM zcds_my_view
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_results)
  FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @LR_range
  WHERE key_field = @LR_range-low
  %_HINTS HDB '&prefer_join_with_fda 1&'.

Why This Works: SAP HANA Fast Data Access (FDA)

FDA is a SAP HANA optimization specifically designed for FOR ALL ENTRIES queries. Instead of expanding the input list into SQL filter conditions, HANA:

  1. Loads the input list into a HANA-managed temporary table in memory.
  2. Joins the CDS view result against this temporary table.
  3. Lets the HANA query optimizer determine the most efficient join strategy.

The result is a single, stable, reusable SQL execution plan regardless of how many entries are in the input list.
Compilation overhead is eliminated, plan cache reuse is maximized, and CPU consumption is substantially reduced.

Comparison: IN vs FOR ALL ENTRIES + FDA

AspectIN '@LR_range'FOR ALL ENTRIES + FDA
SQL generatedOne condition per range entrySingle join against temp table
Compile timeGrows with range sizeConstant
Plan cache reuseLow (unique SQL per execution)High (stable plan)
CPU impact at scaleHighLow
ABAP complexityMinimalSlightly higher

 

Important considerations for FOR ALL ENTRIES: If the input table is empty, FOR ALL ENTRIES returns no rows, unlike IN which would return all rows in that case.
It also implicitly removes duplicate rows from the result set.
Ensure your application logic accounts for both behaviors before applying this change.
 

General Best Practices

Always Test in a Non-Production System First

Both fixes involve changes at the database or ABAP layer that affect execution plans.
Apply in a development or quality system first, execute the affected Fiori apps or programs, verify correct results, and confirm the execution plan has changed as expected via M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE before promoting to production.

Manage the SQL Plan Cache Proactively

After applying any execution hint or changing ABAP logic, clear the relevant SQL Plan Cache entries.
Leaving stale entries in place will delay the effect of any optimization until the cache entry naturally expires.

-- Clear cache entries for a specific CDS view
ALTER SYSTEM REMOVE SQL PLAN CACHE
  WHERE STATEMENT_STRING LIKE '%YOUR_CDS_VIEW_NAME%';

-- Clear cache entries for a specific statement hash
ALTER SYSTEM REMOVE SQL PLAN CACHE
  WHERE STATEMENT_HASH = 'your_statement_hash_here';

Monitor Regularly, Not Just Reactively

Set up routine monitoring for:

  • CPU usage trends over 7-day rolling windows - distinguishes growing load from isolated incidents.
  • Top SQL statements by CPU time via M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE or HANA Cockpit's SQL Monitor.
  • Out-of-Memory dumps - a leading indicator of memory pressure before they become business-impacting outages.

Early detection allows you to address performance issues during planned maintenance windows rather than under incident pressure.

Summary

Both issues share a common theme: code that looks correct and performs acceptably in development causes measurable production impact at scale.
The fixes are targeted, low-risk, and reversible.

IssueRoot CauseSolutionSAP Note
High CPU on CDS views with localeWITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?) prevents HEX engine usageEnable hex_enable_with_parameters_locale (prerequisite), then add USE_HEX_PLAN via @consumption.dbHints (preferred) or ANNOTATION fallback

3513507, 3076534,
2717265

High CPU from large filter listsABAP IN operator generates thousands of SQL conditionsUse FOR ALL ENTRIES with FDA hint2399993

 

References

  • SAP Note 3513507 - HANA HEX Engine not used for queries containing WITH PARAMETERS('LOCALE' = ?)
  • SAP Note 3076534 - Temporary use of ANNOTATION HINT
  • SAP Note 2399993 - FAQ: SAP HANA Fast Data Access (FDA)
  • SAP Note 2717265 - ABAP CDS: Using Database Hints via Annotations (preferred approach for custom CDS views)
  • SAP Help Portal — Accessing Localized Texts in CDS Views
  • SAP HANA Administration Guide — SQL Plan Cache

entry.title: Enhancing Fiori Elements with Contact Quick View in RAP using CDS Annotation.
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

SAP Fiori Elements provides rich UI capabilities through annotations, reducing the need for custom UI development. One such feature is the Contact Quick View, which allows users to view contact information such as name, phone number, and address in a pop-up card directly from a table or object page.

In RAP applications, Contact Quick View can be implemented using CDS view annotations and UI metadata annotations. This blog shows how to create a contact entity and display the contact card in object page sections.

Why Contact Quick View?

A Contact Quick View typically act as a pop-up card that appears when a user clicks on a contact field in a Fiori application. It provides quick access to contact details without navigating to another screen.

Typical information displayed includes:

  • Full Name
  • Phone Number
  • Address
  • Email

This feature improves the user experience by making important contact information readily available.

STEP 1: Create a Contact CDS View.

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Contact entity view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZI_CONTACT
{
  key id            as ID,

  @Semantics.name.fullName: true
  name              as Name,

  @Semantics.telephone.type: [#PREF]
  phone             as Phone,

  @Semantics.eMail.address: true
  email             as Email,

  @Semantics.address.label: true
  address_label     as AddressLabel
}

 

AnnotationPurpose
@Semantics.name.fullNameIdentifies the full name of the contact
@Semantics.telephone.typeDefines the preferred phone number
@Semantics.eMail.addressDefines the eMail address
@Semantics.address.labelAddress description

These semantic annotations enable SAP Fiori Elements to automatically build the Contact Quick View card.

STEP 2: Create an Association to the Contact Entity

In your root CDS view, create an association to the contact entity.

association [0..1] to ZI_CONTACT as _Contact 
on $projection.ContactID = _Contact.ID;

Expose the association:

_Conatct

STEP 3: Display Contact Quick View in Object Page.

To show the contact information in a Field Group or Header Section, use the #AS_CONTACT annotation.

@ui.facet: [ { purpose : #HEADER,
                 label : 'General Data',
                  type : #FIELDGROUP_REFERENCE,
       targetQualifier : 'HeaderData' } ]

@UI.fieldGroup: [ { qualifier : 'HeaderData',
                         type : #AS_CONTACT,
                        label : 'Contact Information',
                     position : 70,
                        value : '_Contact' } ]

When users open the Object Page, they will see a contact field. Clicking it opens the Contact Quick View card displaying all contact details.

To display the contact directly within a List Report table, use the following annotation:

@UI.lineItem: [
  {
    type      : #AS_CONTACT,
    label     : 'Contact',
    value     : '_Contact',
    position  : 120
  }
]

Output:

The Contact Quick View feature offers several advantages:

  • Improved user experience
  • Quick access to contact information
  • Reduced navigation effort
  • Standard SAP Fiori Elements functionality
  • Minimal custom UI development
  • Fully annotation-driven implementation

Key Points

  1. Contact data must be available through a dedicated CDS entity.
  2. Semantic annotations are mandatory for Fiori Elements to recognize contact information.
  3. The association to the contact entity must be exposed in the consumption view.
  4. Use type: #AS_CONTACT in @ui.lineItem for List Reports.
  5. Use type: #AS_CONTACT in @ui.fieldGroup for Object Pages.

Conclusion

Contact Quick View is a powerful Fiori Elements feature that enhances application usability by providing instant access to contact information. By leveraging CDS semantic annotations and the #AS_CONTACT UI annotation, RAP developers can implement this functionality with minimal effort and no custom UI coding.

Using this approach, SAP developers can deliver more interactive and user-friendly applications while fully embracing the annotation-driven development model of RAP and SAP Fiori Elements.

entry.title: Why SAP S/4HANA Transformations Fail When Business Processes Are Automated Before They Are Optimized
entry.content:
Why SAP S/4HANA Transformations Fail When Business Processes Are Automated Before They Are Optimized

Introduction

One of the most common mistakes I have observed during SAP S/4HANA transformation programs is the tendency to automate existing business processes without first evaluating whether those processes are effective.

Organizations often invest significant time and resources implementing automation, workflows, robotic process automation (RPA), AI-powered assistants, and system integrations. However, if the underlying process is inefficient, inconsistent, or poorly designed, automation may simply accelerate existing problems rather than solve them.

Over the years, I have seen organizations achieve greater transformation success when they focus first on process optimization and then on automation.

The Automation Trap

Many transformation programs begin with the goal of reducing manual effort.

While this objective is understandable, teams sometimes assume that every manual activity should be automated.

Questions such as:

  • Can this approval be automated?

  • Can AI make this decision?

  • Can we eliminate manual intervention?

are frequently discussed.

However, a more important question is often overlooked:

Is this process designed correctly in the first place?

Automating an inefficient process rarely creates an efficient outcome.

When Automation Creates New Problems

In one transformation program, multiple approval layers had evolved over several years.

The process contained redundant approvals, overlapping responsibilities, and unnecessary delays.

Rather than simplifying the process, the organization initially focused on automating the approval workflow.

The result was predictable.

Approvals moved electronically instead of manually, but the overall cycle time remained largely unchanged because the process itself was still inefficient.

The organization eventually redesigned the approval structure, eliminated unnecessary steps, clarified ownership, and then automated the optimized workflow.

Only then did measurable improvements occur.

Process Optimization Before Automation

Before automation initiatives begin, organizations should evaluate:

Process Complexity

Does the process contain unnecessary steps?

Process Ownership

Is accountability clearly defined?

Business Value

Does each activity contribute meaningful value?

Standardization

Are different teams following the same process?

Compliance Requirements

Which controls are truly required?

Answering these questions often reveals opportunities to simplify operations before introducing automation.

The Role of SAP S/4HANA

SAP S/4HANA provides powerful capabilities for process standardization and automation.

However, the platform delivers the greatest value when organizations use the transformation as an opportunity to challenge existing practices.

Instead of asking:

"What should we automate?"

Organizations should first ask:

"What should we improve?"

The most successful transformations typically combine process redesign, organizational alignment, and technology modernization.

Where AI Fits Into the Equation

The growing adoption of AI introduces another layer of opportunity.

AI can:

  • Identify process bottlenecks

  • Detect inefficiencies

  • Predict delays

  • Recommend workflow improvements

  • Analyze user behavior patterns

However, AI is most effective when applied to well-defined and optimized business processes.

Organizations that deploy AI on top of inconsistent processes often struggle to achieve expected outcomes.

Signs That a Process Needs Optimization First

Several indicators suggest that optimization should occur before automation:

  • Frequent exceptions

  • Multiple approval layers

  • Duplicate data entry

  • Unclear ownership

  • High rework rates

  • Inconsistent execution across teams

  • Long cycle times

When these symptoms exist, process redesign should generally precede automation initiatives.

Lessons Learned

One lesson I have learned throughout SAP transformation programs is that technology is rarely the root cause of process inefficiency.

Most challenges originate from process design, governance, ownership, or organizational complexity.

Automation can amplify the effectiveness of a good process.

It can also amplify the inefficiencies of a poor one.

The difference lies in whether optimization occurs before automation.

Final Thoughts

SAP S/4HANA transformations provide organizations with a unique opportunity to rethink how work is performed.

Rather than simply digitizing existing processes, organizations should use transformation programs to simplify, standardize, and optimize operations before introducing automation.

In my experience, the most successful SAP programs are not those that automate the most processes. They are the ones that improve the right processes first and then apply automation where it creates meaningful business value.

Optimization before automation is often the difference between transformation and digitized inefficiency.

entry.title: Building a Sales Order Backlog Report Using Custom CDS Views (Sales + Billing)
entry.content:
Amartya Ghosh Dastidar, Senior SAP Developer


Introduction

Core Data Services (CDS) views are central to data modelling in SAP S/4HANA. A frequent requirement in the Sales and Distribution (SD) area is a sales order backlog report — the value and quantity of customer orders that have been placed but not yet invoiced. This post explains how to build such a report using custom CDS views that combine the Sales and Billing domains, covering the key techniques involved: joins, aggregation, calculated fields, type casting, null handling, and the correct treatment of cancelled and pro-forma invoices.

Prerequisites

  • SAP S/4HANA system with ABAP Development Tools (ADT) in Eclipse
  • Basic knowledge of ABAP CDS and SQL
  • Familiarity with SD documents (sales orders, billing documents) and the display transactions VA03 / VF03

Understanding the Sales Order Backlog

In SD, a customer order flows through a chain of documents before revenue is recognised:

Sales Order  --->  Outbound Delivery  --->  Billing Document

            (delivery backlog)        (billing backlog)

The term “backlog” can refer to either of two gaps in this chain:

  1. Delivery backlog — orders placed but not yet delivered.
  2. Billing backlog — orders placed but not yet invoiced.

The report described here implements the billing view, defined as Backlog = Ordered Value − Billed Value. This definition determines the data model, because it requires the Sales domain and the Billing domain to be combined rather than reading open order quantities alone.

Standard CDS Views Used

The report is built on top of two released standard CDS views:

  1. I_SalesOrderItem — sales order line items (ordered quantity and net value).
  2. I_BillingDocumentItem — billing document line items (invoiced quantity and net value), which reference the originating sales order line via SalesDocument and SalesDocumentItem.

Solution Architecture

The solution uses a two-view (layered) design:

  1. ZIC_LIVEBILLING (helper view) — returns only valid, non-cancelled invoices per sales order item.
  2. ZIC_SALES_BACKLOG (main view) — joins the sales order item to the helper, aggregates billing, and calculates the backlog.

The layering is necessary because the cancellation logic requires a self-join that cannot be expressed inline within an aggregating view on all CDS compiler releases. Isolating it in a helper view also keeps the cleaning logic in one place and results in a cleaner, more maintainable model.

Step 1: Create the Helper View (ZIC_LIVEBILLING)

This view filters the billing document items down to genuine, live invoices — excluding pro-forma documents, cancellation documents, and cancelled originals.

Explanation

  1. SDDocumentCategory = 'M' — keeps only invoice-type documents; excludes pro-forma invoices.
  2. CancelledBillingDocument = '' — excludes the cancellation documents themselves.
  3. The self-join on C.CancelledBillingDocument = B.BillingDocument links each invoice to any document that cancelled it. The condition C.BillingDocument is null then keeps only invoices that were never cancelled, removing the cancelled originals.

Step 2: Create the Main View (ZIC_SALES_BACKLOG)

The main view joins the sales order item to the cleaned billing data, aggregates the billed values, and derives the backlog.

Key Techniques Explained

  1. Aggregation with GROUP BY — a single order line can be billed across several invoices (partial billing). Summing the billing per order key collapses these into one row and prevents the ordered value from being multiplied.
  2. Type casting — currency amounts of different internal lengths cannot be subtracted directly. Casting all amounts to abap.curr(23,2) (and quantities to abap.quan(13,3)) ensures type compatibility.
  3. COALESCE for null handling — an order line with no invoice returns null from the join; coalesce(..., 0) converts null to zero so the arithmetic remains valid.
  4. Calculated fields — the backlog quantity and value are derived inline as ordered minus billed.
  5. Session date — dats_days_between(RequestedDeliveryDate, $session.system_date) produces the number of days a line is overdue, enabling aging analysis.

Business Logic: Counting Only Valid Billing

Accurate backlog depends on summing only genuine, live invoices. Three categories must be excluded:

  1. Pro-forma invoices — not real sales; excluded via SDDocumentCategory = 'M'.
  2. Cancelled invoices — a cancelled invoice and its cancellation document both remain in the table; the self-join in the helper view removes both, leaving only the live invoice.
  3. Rejected order items — lines with a rejection reason are excluded via SalesDocumentRjcnReason = ''.

Note: the field that stores the cancellation reference can vary by release and may sit on the cancellation document rather than the original. The self-join approach is robust to this, because it identifies cancelled documents from the relationship rather than from a single status field.

Validation

The view should be validated against the source documents (VA03, VF03, and document flow) across the main data patterns:

  • Never billed — no invoice in the flow; backlog equals the full order value.
  • Partially billed — invoice covers part of the order; backlog equals the remaining value and quantity.
  • Fully billed — invoice equals the order; backlog is zero.
  • Cancelled invoice — only the live invoice is counted; backlog reflects the remaining value.

Where source data itself is inconsistent (for example a negatively priced item or a mis-keyed invoice amount), the view reflects the source faithfully. A reporting view should not silently correct source data; such cases are data-quality issues to be flagged separately.

Standard vs Custom CDS Views

Standard delivery/open-order backlog content is appropriate where a vanilla delivery backlog with standard fields is required. A custom view is justified when the requirement differs, as in this case:

  • The metric is ordered − billed (a billing backlog), which standard content does not frame the same way.
  • It combines Sales and Billing into one pre-shaped row with the specific fields and aging logic required for reporting.
  • It encodes system-specific billing rules (pro-forma and cancellation handling) validated against actual documents.

The trade-off is ongoing maintenance. Where standard content satisfies the same definition, it should be preferred to reduce maintenance overhead.

Key Points to Remember

  • Clarify the backlog definition (delivery vs billing) before designing the view.
  • The SQL view name is limited to 16 characters.
  • Aggregate one-to-many relationships before joining to avoid duplication.
  • Cast all amounts and quantities to a common type before arithmetic.
  • Exclude pro-forma, cancelled, and rejected documents from billed totals.
  • Use a layered design when complex filtering cannot be expressed inline.
  • Validate the output against source documents before release.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Business Partner Address Management
entry.content:
Business Partner Address 
 
In SAP S/4HANA, the Business Partner (BP) acts as a centralized master data object. It serves as a single point of entry for managing all master data related to Customers, Vendors, and Suppliers. 

Unlike the older SAP ECC system, which requires separate transactions (XD01, VD01, XK01, etc.) to maintain separate customer and vendor master data, the Business Partner model in S/4HANA consolidates these entries. This helps avoid data redundancy and streamlines transaction processing. 

When we look into Business Partner Addresses, we are specifically dealing with the geographic location and communication contact details maintained for that particular BP. 

Major Types of Business Partners 

There are three primary categories of Business Partners in SAP : 

  • Person: Refers to an individual (e.g., a specific employee, private customer, or contact person). 
  • Organization: Refers to a legal entity or company (e.g., a corporate client, vendor, or subsidiary). 
  • Group: Refers to a collection of persons or organizations (e.g., a household or a buying consortium). 

 When it comes to Business Partner Address handling, the behavior is more or less similar across all BP types. 

However, there is a minor difference between Person BP and Organization BP. 
 
When creating an Organization BP, an address number is mandatory and automatically assigned to the data record.  

However, for a Person BP, a physical address number is not strictly mandatory at the time of creation. Instead, a unique person number is generated in the BUT000 table, which points to person-centric address details stored in the ADRP table. 

 
In the below Screenshot, have created a Person BP without Address. 

If you see the BP entry in table BUT000 (General Business Partner Data), you can observe the generated Person Number. 

Maintaining a Business Partner Address 

When creating or updating a Business Partner address, the following location details are generally maintained: 

  • Street 
  • House Number 
  • Postal Code 
  • City 
  • Country 
  • Region 

 
Mandatory Field Validation 

Country is a strictly mandatory field. 

If the Country field is not maintained, SAP throws the following standard validation error:  
"Address is incomplete; please enter Country/Region". 
 

Once the address is successfully maintained and saved, it becomes visible under the Address Overview tab of the Business Partner master data. 

Address Validity and the Standard Address 

 By default, when a new address is created, the system sets its validity period from the Date of Creation until the end of technical time (31/12/9999). This first valid address automatically becomes the Standard Address. 

 

Important Rule 

Every active Business Partner must contain at least one valid Standard Address. 

If the validity of the Standard Address expires and no new valid address exists, the system throws an error during transaction processing. 
The Standard Address is used as the default address in standard SAP transactions. 
 

The created address entries can also be seen in backend table BUT020. 

Communication Types in Business Partner 

Within a Business Partner record, communication details (such as Mobile Number, Telephone, Email Address, and Fax) are split into two categories: 

  1. Independent Communication 
  1. Dependent Communication 

 

Independent Communication 

These contact details are linked directly to the Business Partner as an entity, independent of any physical address. 

Example: Your personal mobile number or personal email ID that travels with you regardless of which office location you sit in. 

These communication details remain valid even if the physical address changes. 
 

The Address Number for Independent Communication is stored in table BUT000 under field ADDRCOMM. 

Since it is directly linked to the Business Partner, the communication reference is available in the General Business Partner Data table itself. 

To view the actual Email Address: 

  • Copy the ADDRCOMM value from BUT000. 
  • Search the same Address Number in table ADR6. 

 

Dependent Communication 

These contact details are explicitly linked to a specific physical address. 
 
Example: Imagine you are an employee at XYZ Company. You have a company-specific email ID linked to the corporate headquarters address. If you change companies or if that corporate office address changes, your company-specific email address becomes invalid, whereas your personal/independent communication details remain active. 
If the physical address changes or becomes invalid, the communication details associated with that address may also become invalid. 
 

The Address Number for Dependent Communication is stored in table BUT020. 

Since it is linked to a specific address, we need to fetch the Address Number from the BP Address table. 

To view the actual Email Address: 

  • Copy the Address Number from BUT020. 
  • Search the same Address Number in table ADR6.

 
Multiple Addresses & Address Usages 

A Single Business Partner can hold multiple addresses concurrently (e.g., a person can have a Residential Address, a Permanent Address, and a Work/Company Address). 

To manage how these addresses behave, SAP uses Address Usages. 

Examples: 

  • Billing Address 
  • Ship-To Address 
  • Standard Address 

A single address can simultaneously serve multiple purposes. 

Creating Multiple Addresses 

To create multiple addresses: 

  • Navigate to the Address Overview tab. 
  • Click on the Create button. 

Example, if we want to create a Billing Address: 

  • Double-click on Billing Address under Address Usages. 
  • Maintain the required address details. 

Note: SAP allows multiple addresses for the same usage type through SPRO configuration. 
 
SPRO Path 

SPRO → Cross-Application Components → SAP Business Partner → Business Partner → Basic Settings → Address Determination → Address Types 

Under this configuration, SAP provides a checkbox called Multiple Usages

If this checkbox is enabled: 

Multiple physical addresses can be assigned to the same Address Usage type.

 

All assigned Address Usages are stored in backend table BUT021_FS.

Address Programmatic Interface (BAPIs) 

When manipulating Business Partner addresses via custom ABAP code or interface developments, you should avoid direct database updates. Instead, use the following standard BAPIs: 

  • BUPA_ADDRESS_ADD — To append a new address to an existing BP. 
  • BUPA_ADDRESS_CHANGE — To modify existing address lines or fields. 
  • BUPA_ADDRESS_REMOVE — To delete or clear an address from a BP record. Etc... 

Conclusion 

Understanding SAP Business Partner Address Management and its backend table relationships is very important for both technical and functional consultants working in SAP S/4HANA environments. 

Having proper knowledge of: 

  • Address Validity 
  • Standard Address 
  • Independent and Dependent Communication 
  • Multiple Address Usage 
  • Backend Table Relationships 
  • Standard BP Address BAPIs 

helps significantly during: 

  • Support activities 
  • Incident analysis 
  • Debugging 
  • Interface development 
  • Custom enhancements 

I hope this blog provides a useful understanding of SAP Business Partner Address concepts and helps consultants working on real-time SAP BP scenarios.

entry.title: Why SAP ChaRM Still Matters in the S/4HANA Era: Lessons from Large-Scale Transformation Programs
entry.content:
Why SAP ChaRM Still Matters in the S/4HANA Era: Lessons from Large-Scale Transformation Programs

SAP ChaRM Change Lifecycle

Introduction

In many SAP transformation programs, project teams focus heavily on system design, data migration, testing, and cutover planning. However, one critical capability often receives attention only after deployment issues begin to appear: Change and Release Management.

Over the years, I have worked on multiple large SAP transformation programs where thousands of transport requests moved across development, quality, and production environments. While organizations invest heavily in implementation methodologies, many still underestimate the operational importance of a structured transport governance framework.

This is where SAP Solution Manager Change Request Management (ChaRM) continues to play a significant role.

Although SAP Cloud ALM is becoming increasingly popular, many organizations running ECC and SAP S/4HANA environments still depend on ChaRM to maintain governance, compliance, auditability, and release control.

In my experience, the difference between a stable deployment and a chaotic production rollout often comes down to how effectively change management processes are implemented.

What is SAP ChaRM?

SAP Change Request Management (ChaRM) is a capability within SAP Solution Manager that helps organizations manage the lifecycle of system changes from request initiation through deployment.

ChaRM provides:

• Change Request Tracking

• Transport Management

• Approval Workflows

• Release Governance

• Audit Compliance

• Emergency Change Management

• Deployment Documentation

Instead of manually tracking transports across spreadsheets, emails, and meetings, ChaRM creates a centralized governance framework that controls how changes move through the SAP landscape.

The Challenge Most SAP Programs Face

During a global SAP S/4HANA transformation, hundreds of developers, functional consultants, integration teams, and testing teams may be working simultaneously.

Without a structured governance process, organizations frequently encounter:

• Unauthorized transports

• Incomplete testing

• Emergency fixes bypassing controls

• Conflicting releases

• Missing approvals

• Audit findings

• Production instability

These issues are rarely caused by technology limitations. They are usually process and governance failures.

A Real Program Lesson

During one enterprise transformation program, multiple workstreams were preparing for a major release involving:

• Finance

• Supply Chain

• Manufacturing

• Procurement

• Integration platforms

As deployment approached, transport volume increased significantly.

Without proper release governance, several transport dependencies were identified only days before production deployment.

The project team spent considerable effort reconciling transport conflicts, identifying missing approvals, and validating deployment sequences.

The lesson was clear:

The risk was not the transport itself.

The risk was the lack of visibility into the overall change landscape.

Organizations need a governance framework that provides traceability from requirement to deployment.

This is precisely the problem ChaRM was designed to solve.

Why ChaRM Matters for SAP S/4HANA Programs

1. Improved Governance

Every transport becomes associated with:

• Business requirement

• Approval records

• Testing evidence

• Deployment documentation

This creates complete traceability across the implementation lifecycle.

2. Stronger Audit Compliance

Many regulated industries require evidence that production changes followed approved processes.

ChaRM automatically captures:

• Approvals

• Change history

• Deployment records

• Transport ownership

This significantly simplifies audit preparation.

3. Better Release Management

Large transformation programs often deploy multiple releases simultaneously.

ChaRM helps teams:

• Bundle related transports

• Manage release schedules

• Reduce deployment conflicts

• Improve release predictability

4. Emergency Change Control

Production incidents inevitably occur.

The challenge is balancing speed with governance.

ChaRM supports emergency transport processes while maintaining visibility and auditability.

This helps organizations resolve critical issues without sacrificing compliance.

Common Mistakes Organizations Make

Over the years, I have observed several recurring mistakes:

Treating ChaRM as an IT Tool

Successful organizations treat ChaRM as a governance framework, not merely a transport management solution.

Excessive Customization

Overengineering approval workflows often introduces unnecessary complexity.

The objective should be governance efficiency, not process bureaucracy.

Ignoring Business Ownership

Change governance should involve both IT and business stakeholders.

Production changes ultimately impact business operations, not just technical systems.

Delaying ChaRM Implementation

Many programs wait until testing phases to implement change governance.

By that stage, transport volume and complexity have already increased significantly.

ChaRM vs Cloud ALM

As SAP continues investing in Cloud ALM, organizations frequently ask whether ChaRM remains relevant.

The answer depends on organizational maturity, landscape complexity, and regulatory requirements.

For many enterprises running hybrid environments, ChaRM continues to provide robust governance capabilities that remain critical for large-scale deployments.

The focus should not be selecting a tool.

The focus should be establishing a disciplined change management process that supports reliable business operations.

Final Thoughts

Technology transformations succeed when governance evolves alongside technology.

Organizations invest millions in SAP S/4HANA programs, yet production stability often depends on something far less visible: disciplined change management.

SAP ChaRM may not be the most discussed component of an SAP transformation, but in many programs it serves as the foundation that connects requirements, testing, approvals, transports, and production deployment.

From my experience, successful transformation programs do not simply manage transports.

They manage change.

And that distinction often determines whether a deployment becomes a success story or a post-go-live recovery effort.

entry.title: Intel and SAP Deepen Collaboration for SAP HANA Cloud Co-Development Initiative
entry.content:
A new chapter of co-engineering around SAP HANA Cloud, focused on cloud-native scale, performance, and what’s next for customers.


Disclaimer: This blogpost has been co-published at community.intel.com.

Introduction

Intel and SAP are expanding their long-standing collaboration through a dedicated SAP HANA Cloud co-development initiative, bringing engineering teams closer together to help SAP HANA Cloud run even better on current and future Intel platforms. This is the start of a new chapter in how Intel and SAP co-innovate: aligning SAP’s product direction with Intel’s platform roadmap, so optimizations show up where they matter most, in real customer environments running on leading cloud service providers.

A new chapter of co-engineering for SAP HANA Cloud

This initiative is founded on the principle that early and comprehensive collaboration between platform and software engineering teams leads to enhanced customer experiences. The ongoing partnership has demonstrated great benefits for SAP HANA during the last decade.

This collaboration creates a direct technical bridge between SAP product development and Intel architecture/platform roadmaps, helping ensure new capabilities and platform features and enhancements translate into real, production-ready outcomes across SAP's certified cloud service providers.

Value Across the Ecosystem

The co-development initiative is designed to deliver clear value across all stakeholders:

For SAP: Architectural optimizations for SAP HANA Cloud features, accelerated technology adoption, and reduced risk during transitions, such as migrating to new Intel Xeon generations, introduce new and/or enhanced Intel features.

For Intel: Ensuring SAP HANA Cloud runs at its best on Intel x86 Architectures and maintains and improves competitiveness against alternative architectures. Strengthening Intel's position for SAP's strategic cloud deployments at the Cloud Service Providers (CSPs).

For Customers and CSPs: Improved price-performance, efficiency, scalability, and resiliency are direct benefits from joint SAP-Intel developments in both performance and feature sets.

What we’re building together

Intel and SAP share a vision to proactively optimize SAP HANA Cloud on current and future Intel platforms, delivering value for SAP, cloud service providers, and, most importantly, customers running mission-critical workloads on SAP HANA Cloud. By connecting SAP product development with Intel architecture and platform roadmaps, the teams are creating an engine for continuous optimization, early validation, and faster production readiness.

Where we’ll focus first

The initiative is organized around three practical workstreams designed to turn joint engineering into customer-impacting outcomes:

Platform Development

Intel and SAP will work together on feature and workload optimization across key SAP HANA Cloud scenarios, such as OLTP, OLAP, scale-up, and scale-out, while also streamlining the introduction of new Intel® Xeon® processor-based cloud instances and their new capabilities.

Performance Benchmarking

The teams will jointly review SAP HANA Cloud–specific benchmarks and tune for strong, repeatable results on Intel platforms at CSPs, addressing constraints observed in production and helping ensure competitive performance compared with alternative architectures. This applies to Transactional, Analytical and AI-enhanced workloads.

Product Readiness

Intel will help SAP accelerate the adoption of new instance types and configurations across SAP HANA Cloud editions and ensure that proven optimizations carry forward smoothly over time, so customers benefit consistently throughout the service lifecycle.

 

What does this mean for customers and cloud service providers for SAP solutions?

For customers, this initiative is about turning platform innovation into practical outcomes, better price-performance, better efficiency, and faster readiness for what comes next. For cloud service providers, it’s about tighter alignment and earlier validation so SAP HANA Cloud can take advantage of new Intel capabilities in real production environments.

Looking ahead: stay tuned

As this co-development effort ramps up, SAP HANA Cloud is positioned to benefit from optimizations on Intel Xeon platforms, including Intel Xeon 6 processors and future generations. The focus is straightforward: accelerate production readiness, scale and efficiency, and deliver continuous improvements that customers can use, without disruption.

This is just the beginning. Intel and SAP are deepening co-engineering for SAP HANA Cloud and Cloud Native Architectures at SAP to help SAP to scale, perform, and evolve on Intel platforms as SAP Cloud ERP adoption accelerates. Stay tuned for the innovations Intel and SAP will deliver to customers, and for more updates as the teams turn this initiative into tangible improvements across cloud environments.

 

 

 

Notices and Disclaimers

Performance varies by use, configuration, and other factors. Learn more on the Performance Index site. 

Performance results are based on testing as of dates shown in configurations and may not reflect all publicly available ​updates.  See backup for configuration details.  No product or component can be absolutely secure.

Your costs and results may vary.

Intel technologies may require enabled hardware, software, or service activation.

© Intel Corporation.  Intel, the Intel logo, and other Intel marks are trademarks of Intel Corporation or its subsidiaries.  Other names and brands may be claimed as the property of others.

entry.title: Beyond SAP BW: How SAP HANA Native SQL and Google Cloud Are Redefining Enterprise Analytics
entry.content:
This blog explores the swift transition from legacy BW bottlenecks to cloud velocity, highlighting how modern data platforms can improve performance, scalability, agility, and business insight delivery while reducing unnecessary data movement, storage and processing cost.

Modern SAP BW: Challenges and Limitations

Enterprise reporting environments are under increasing pressure to deliver faster insights, reduce technical complexity, and support real-time decision-making. Traditional SAP BW architectures have served businesses well for many years, but many organisations are now facing challenges around long-running process chains, duplicated data, complex transformations, high storage costs, and delayed reporting.

Legacy SAP BW environments often contain multiple layers of extraction, staging, transformation, aggregation, and reporting logic. Over time, this can create a complex and costly landscape. Common challenges include:

  • Heavy BW transformations that increase maintenance effort.
  • Long-running process chains that delay business reporting.
  • Data duplication across PSA, DSO, InfoCubes, aggregates, and reporting layers.
  • Limited real-time analytics capability.
  • Complex ETL pipelines that reduce business agility.
  • Higher infrastructure and operational costs.

Figure 1:

SAP BW – Challenges & limitations

To address these challenges, a modern enterprise data strategy should focus on moving computation closer to the data. This is where SAP HANA Native SQL Data Warehouse becomes a powerful alternative. Instead of relying on heavy SAP BW transformations and batch-driven reporting processes, SAP HANA can perform the heavy processing directly where the data resides.

The future-state architecture is built around a modern, real-time, SAP HANA-native data platform with API-first access.

The proposed architecture consists of five major layers:

  1. Data Sources
    These include SAP S/4HANA, SAP ECC, SAP BW legacy data, POS/store systems, eCommerce, CRM/loyalty, supply chain, finance, and external third-party data.
  2. Ingest and Replication Layer
    SAP SLT can be used for real-time change data capture and replication. SAP SDI and SDA can support advanced integration and federated access where required.
  3. SAP HANA Native SQL Data Warehouse
    SAP HANA becomes the core processing layer. It handles SQL pushdown, joins, aggregations, calculations, business rules, and high-performance analytics.
  4. Semantic and Service Layer
    SAP CAP exposes governed services, APIs, OData endpoints, REST APIs, and business services. This layer makes the data secure, reusable, and consumable.
  5. Consumption Layer
    Business users and platforms consume curated datasets through tools such as Power BI, Tableau, SAP Analytics Cloud, Dataiku, Python/Jupyter, Vertex AI, BigQuery, Looker, and custom applications.

This architecture allows SAP HANA to perform what it does best, Move computation to the data, not data to computation. This allows the SAP HANA DB to depict its fast in-memory processing, while cloud and analytics platforms focus on advanced analytics, machine learning, reporting, and business consumption.

The SAP HANA Database should be responsible for the heavy lifting. This includes:

  • Large joins across high-volume datasets.
  • Aggregations across stores, products, customers, and time periods.
  • Real-time business calculations.
  • Business rules and data transformations.
  • Parallel processing for analytical workloads.
  • SQL views, calculation views, table functions, and SQLScript logic.

Figure 2:

Future-State Enterprise Data Architecture

The below example depicts an example of a Sales and Gross Margin report, which is processed directly in SAP HANA using SQL pushdown logic. Instead of extracting 100 million detailed rows into another platform, SAP HANA can aggregate /union the data first and send only the curated business-ready result, such as 500,000 rows, to downstream consumers. This reduces data movement, improves performance, lowers storage requirements, and supports faster reporting:

Figure 3:

Sales and Gross Margin report using SQL pushdown logic

The proposed integration strategy between SAP and Google Cloud Platform follows a clean separation of responsibilities:

SAP source systems provide operational data such as sales orders, materials, customers, inventory, pricing, and transactions. SAP SLT performs near real-time replication into SAP HANA. SAP HANA then performs SQL pushdown, joins, calculations, aggregations, and business-rule processing.

Only curated, cleansed, validated, and business-ready datasets should be sent to GCP. Once data reaches Google Cloud, platforms such as Cloud Storage, Dataproc, BigQuery, Looker, Dataiku, and Vertex AI can focus on analytics, orchestration, machine learning, and advanced data science use cases.

Benefits of using SAP HANA pushdown before sending data to cloud platforms (Dataproc and Cloud Storage):

  1. It reduces the volume of data written to cloud storage sinks. Instead of repeatedly writing large raw datasets, only the required aggregated and curated data is transferred.
  2. It improves Dataproc and Spark execution. When Dataproc receives smaller, cleaner, and better-structured datasets, it spends less time performing expensive shuffle operations and heavy transformations.
  3. It reduces cloud storage consumption and duplication. This helps lower operational costs and avoids unnecessary replication of large data volumes.
  4. It enables near real-time analytics. Business teams can access fresher data for dashboards, reports, data science models, and operational decision-making.
  5. It reduces network transfer and operational cost by avoiding unnecessary movement of detailed raw data between platforms.

Figure 4:

Benefits For Dataproc and Cloud Storage

SAP CAP plays an important role in the modern architecture, but it should not become the new transformation engine but rather focus on the below role:

  • Expose APIs.
  • Manage service definitions.
  • Provide OData and REST endpoints.
  • Apply authentication, authorisation, and role-based access control.
  • Handle light business rules and validations.
  • Provide a secure service layer for applications and integrations.

SAP HANA ETL & Replication Tools

Selecting the right SAP HANA integration tool depends on the specific requirements of the data pipeline, including whether the use case requires real-time replication, advanced transformation, federated access, orchestration, or hybrid cloud integration.

SAP SLT is best for real-time replication and change data capture from SAP source systems into SAP HANA.

SAP SDI is useful for advanced ETL, data transformation, heterogeneous source integration, data quality, and batch or near real-time movement.

SAP SDA supports federated virtual access, allowing SAP HANA to access remote data without physically moving it.

SAP Data Intelligence supports orchestration, data pipelines, workflow automation, streaming, AI/ML lifecycle management, metadata management, and hybrid or multi-cloud scenarios.

Figure 5:

SAP HANA ETL & Replication Tools

Enterprise Data Warehouse Layers

A strong SAP HANA Native SQL Data Warehouse should be organised into clear enterprise layers:

  1. The Raw/Landing Layer stores replicated data as-is for auditability and traceability.
  2. The Harmonized Layer standardises, cleanses, validates, and conforms data across systems.
  3. The Business Transformed Layer applies business rules, calculations, master data enrichment, and enterprise logic.
  4. The Analytical Marts Layer creates curated, optimised datasets for reporting and analytics.
  5. The Semantic/API Layer exposes governed data through CDS views, OData services, REST APIs, SOAP/WSDL where required, and secure authorisation.

Across all layers, governance and operations must be embedded. This includes data governance, lineage, data quality, monitoring, metadata management, security, compliance, and access control.

Figure 6:

Enterprise Data Warehouse Layers

Monitoring and Observability: SAP HANA Cockpit and Google Dataproc UI

Monitoring is a critical part of the target architecture because it ensures that both the SAP HANA processing layer and the Google Cloud scaling layer remain stable, visible, and optimised. SAP HANA Cockpit monitors the database engine, while Dataproc UI monitors the cloud-scale processing engine:

SAP HANA Cockpit monitors the SAP HANA Native SQL Data Warehouse. Its responsibility is to track database health, memory usage, CPU usage, disk usage, workload behaviour, expensive SQL statements, long-running queries, alerts, and performance bottlenecks. This ensures that SQL pushdown, joins, aggregations, calculation views, and business logic are running efficiently before curated datasets are sent to Google Cloud:

Figure 7:

SAP HANA Cockpit

For a more detailed explanation of SAP HANA monitoring capabilities, the related SAP Community blog:https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sap-hana-platform-monitoring-analysis/ba-p/13515290  provides additional insight into real-time monitoring and analysis within the SAP HANA platform.

 

Google Dataproc UI monitors the cloud processing layer. Its responsibility is to track Dataproc clusters, Spark/Hadoop jobs, job status, execution progress, failures, logs, and resource behaviour after curated data reaches GCP. This helps confirm whether downstream cloud processing is efficient, scalable, and cost-effective.

Figure 8:

Google Dataproc UI

 

By utilising SAP HANA Native SQL Data Warehouse as the core processing layer, organisations can reduce complexity, improve performance, enable real-time analytics, and integrate more effectively with cloud platforms such as Google Cloud Platform. This target architecture is not simply about replacing one reporting system with another. It is about moving beyond legacy reporting constraints and building a more agile, scalable, governed, and intelligent enterprise data platform. The future of enterprise analytics will belong to organisations that can process trusted data in real time, expose insights securely, and deliver the right information to the right people, tools, and platforms, when it matters most.

In the journey from BW bottlenecks to cloud velocity, SAP HANA becomes the engine, Google Cloud becomes the scale, and trusted real-time insight becomes the competitive advantage.

Thank you all for taking a glimpse into the above Content. Please don’t forget to like, comment, and share.

Abbreviations and Acronyms:

AI

Artificial Intelligence

API

Application Programming Interface

BI

Business Intelligence

BW

Business Warehouse

CAP

Cloud Application Programming Model

CDS

Core Data Services

CRM

Customer Relationship Management

DB

Database

DSO

DataStore Object

ETL

Extract, Transform, Load

GCP

Google Cloud Platform

ML

Machine Learning

OData

Open Data Protocol

POS

Point of Sale

PSA

Persistent Staging Area

REST

Representational State Transfer

SAP S/4HANA

SAP Business Suite 4 SAP HANA

SDA

Smart Data Access

SDI

Smart Data Integration

SLT

SAP Landscape Transformation Replication Server

SOAP

Simple Object Access Protocol

SQL

Structured Query Language

UI

User Interface

WSDL

Web Services Description Language

entry.title: How I Used Microsoft Copilot and Azure Services to Improve SAP S/4HANA Project Tracking
entry.content:
How I Used Microsoft Copilot and Azure Services to Improve SAP S/4HANA Project Tracking and Governance

Introduction

One of the biggest challenges in large SAP S/4HANA transformation programs is not the implementation itself—it is maintaining visibility across hundreds of project activities, risks, defects, decisions, and action items spread across multiple teams and systems.

During global SAP transformation programs, project information is often distributed across several platforms:

  • Azure DevOps for defects and sprint activities

  • Microsoft Project for schedules

  • ServiceNow for incidents and change requests

  • Microsoft Teams for collaboration

  • SharePoint for project documentation

  • SAP Solution Manager or Cloud ALM for project execution activities

As project managers, we spend significant time consolidating information from these sources before we can make informed decisions.

Recently, I explored how Microsoft Copilot combined with Azure services can help project managers improve governance, risk management, reporting, and executive visibility throughout an SAP transformation lifecycle.

The Traditional Project Tracking Challenge

In a typical SAP S/4HANA transformation, a weekly status review often requires collecting information from multiple workstreams:

  • Program Management Office

  • Data Migration Team

  • Integration Team

  • Testing Team

  • Security Team

  • Change Management Team

  • Business Process Owners

The challenge is that critical project information exists in multiple repositories.

Typical Information Landscape

Azure DevOps
   │
   ├── Defects
   ├── User Stories
   └── Sprint Progress

Microsoft Project
   │
   ├── Project Schedule
   ├── Milestones
   └── Dependencies

ServiceNow
   │
   ├── Incidents
   ├── Changes
   └── Service Requests

Microsoft Teams
   │
   ├── Meetings
   ├── Discussions
   └── Action Items

SharePoint
   │
   ├── Documents
   ├── RAID Logs
   └── Status Reports

As a result, project managers spend considerable effort gathering information instead of analyzing risks and making decisions.

Introducing Microsoft Copilot into SAP Project Governance

Microsoft Copilot provides an opportunity to transform project management activities from manual reporting to intelligent project governance.

Rather than searching through multiple systems, project leaders can ask questions in natural language such as:

  • Which workstreams are currently behind schedule?

  • What are the highest-priority risks this week?

  • Which defects remain open beyond SLA?

  • What actions were assigned during steering committee meetings?

  • Which milestones are at risk for the upcoming release?

Instead of manually preparing reports, Copilot can help summarize information from connected project repositories.

Architecture Overview

The following conceptual architecture demonstrates how Azure services and Copilot can support SAP project tracking.

Architecture Diagram

+---------------------------------------------------+
|              SAP Transformation Program           |
+---------------------------------------------------+

          Data Sources
-----------------------------------------------------

 Azure DevOps      Microsoft Project
      |                    |
      |                    |
      +----------+---------+
                 |
                 |
 ServiceNow   SharePoint   Teams
      |           |          |
      +-----------+----------+
                  |
                  v

+--------------------------------------+
|      Microsoft Azure Services        |
+--------------------------------------+
| Azure Data Factory                   |
| Azure Logic Apps                     |
| Azure AI Services                    |
| Azure Storage                        |
| Azure Data Lake                      |
+--------------------------------------+
                  |
                  v

+--------------------------------------+
|         Microsoft Copilot            |
+--------------------------------------+
| Risk Summarization                   |
| Defect Trend Analysis                |
| Executive Status Reporting           |
| Action Item Tracking                 |
| Meeting Summaries                    |
+--------------------------------------+
                  |
                  v

+--------------------------------------+
| SAP Program Leadership Dashboard     |
+--------------------------------------+
| PMO                                  |
| Steering Committee                   |
| Business Stakeholders                |
| Program Leadership                   |
+--------------------------------------+

Use Case 1: Automated Executive Status Reporting

One of the most time-consuming activities for project managers is preparing executive status reports.

Traditionally, project managers gather information from multiple teams and manually create PowerPoint presentations.

With Copilot, project leaders can generate summaries based on:

  • Open risks

  • Defect trends

  • Milestone completion

  • Change requests

  • Resource constraints

Example prompt:

"Summarize all high-priority risks, critical defects, and delayed milestones for the SAP S/4HANA transformation program during the past seven days."

This significantly reduces manual reporting effort and allows project managers to focus on decision-making rather than document preparation.

Use Case 2: Risk Monitoring and Early Warning Indicators

Risk management is critical during SAP transformation programs.

In one project, recurring defects appeared during integration testing across multiple regions. Individually, each defect seemed manageable, but collectively they indicated a broader data quality issue.

Using AI-assisted analysis, project teams can identify:

  • Recurring defect patterns

  • Escalating risks

  • Delayed action items

  • Resource bottlenecks

  • Testing readiness concerns

Example Risk Analysis Flow

Defect Data
      +
Meeting Notes
      +
Action Items
      +
Schedule Updates
      |
      v

Azure AI Analysis

      |
      v

Risk Pattern Detection

      |
      v

Copilot Summary

      |
      v

Project Leadership Alert

This approach helps identify potential problems before they impact major milestones.

Use Case 3: Meeting Action Tracking

A common challenge in SAP programs is following up on action items generated during workshops and governance meetings.

Copilot can help by:

  • Summarizing meeting discussions

  • Extracting action items

  • Identifying owners

  • Tracking due dates

  • Highlighting overdue actions

Instead of manually reviewing meeting notes, project managers receive a consolidated action dashboard.

Use Case 4: Defect Trend Analysis

During testing cycles, hundreds or even thousands of defects may be logged.

Copilot can assist by identifying:

  • Defect hotspots

  • Repeated root causes

  • Process areas generating the highest defect volume

  • Regional implementation concerns

This helps testing managers prioritize corrective actions more effectively.

Lessons Learned

Through my experience managing enterprise transformation programs, I have learned that AI does not replace project managers.

Instead, it enhances their ability to:

  • Process information faster

  • Identify risks earlier

  • Improve governance visibility

  • Reduce manual reporting effort

  • Focus on strategic decision-making

The most successful approach combines:

  • Human expertise

  • Business process knowledge

  • Governance discipline

  • AI-assisted insights

Conclusion

The future of SAP project management is not about replacing project managers with AI. It is about empowering project leaders with better visibility and faster access to information.

By combining Microsoft Copilot with Azure services, organizations can transform project tracking from a manual reporting exercise into a proactive governance capability.

For SAP S/4HANA transformation programs, this means improved transparency, faster decision-making, better risk management, and ultimately a higher probability of successful program delivery.

As SAP transformations continue to grow in complexity, AI-powered project governance will become an increasingly important capability for program leaders seeking to deliver successful business outcomes.

entry.title: Going Beyond the Tip of the Iceberg with SAP HANA Cloud SQL on Files
entry.content:
Related Blogs:
  • Unlocking the True Potential of Data in Files with SAP HANA Database SQL on Files in SAP HANA Cloud
  • Exploring New SQL on Files Use Cases with SAP HANA Cloud QRC 04/2024 and QRC 01/2025
  • Accelerating Your Analytics with Delta Table Replication into SAP HANA Cloud

As I mentioned in my previous blogs, SQL on Files, a capability of SAP HANA database in SAP HANA Cloud, has been continuously expanding since SAP HANA Cloud QRC 3/2024. We started with native read-only access to CSV, Parquet, and Delta tables in SAP HANA Cloud, data lake Files. We then extended this access to external object storages (Amazon S3, Azure Blob/ADLS Gen2, Google Cloud Storage) and external Delta Sharing in QRC 4/2024. Most recently, we introduced Delta table-to-HANA replication in QRC 4/2025.

With SAP HANA Cloud QRC 2/2026, I'm pleased to announce that the journey continues, and this time the spotlight is on Apache Iceberg, the industry-standard open table format that has rapidly become a cornerstone of modern data lakehouse architectures. While Iceberg support has been quietly maturing in SAP HANA Cloud over the last few quarters, this is the first blog dedicated to telling the full story end-to-end.

In a nutshell, here is what SAP HANA Cloud now offers for Apache Iceberg, cumulatively from QRC 3/2025 through QRC 2/2026:

  • Direct read-only access to Apache Iceberg tables in object storage via the file adapter, including time-travel queries (QRC 3/2025).
  • Snapshot replica toggling support for Apache Iceberg virtual tables (QRC 1/2026).
  • Data Page V2 (DATA_PAGE_V2) support in the Parquet reader, aligning SAP HANA Cloud with the latest Parquet ecosystem (QRC 1/2026).
  • Direct read-only access to Apache Iceberg REST Catalogs via the new icebergcatalog adapter. QRC 1/2026 introduced the adapter with Cloudera and Databricks running on AWS as the initially certified providers, and QRC 2/2026 extends the multi-cloud coverage to Azure and GCP for Databricks while additionally certifying Snowflake across AWS, Azure, and GCP. Cloudera certification on Azure and GCP is planned for a future release.
  • AliCloud Object Storage Service (OSS) added to the list of supported external object storages (QRC 2/2026).

Alright, with these points in mind, let's dive deeper into the details with some examples.

 

Apache Iceberg via the file Adapter

Starting with SAP HANA Cloud QRC 3/2025, SQL on Files began supporting Apache Iceberg as an additional file format on top of the existing CSV, Parquet, and Delta table support. As highlighted in my first SQL on Files blog and follow-up blog, the file adapter provides direct read-only access to files in SAP HANA Cloud, data lake Files or in supported external object storages. With Iceberg now in the mix, customers can query Iceberg tables sitting on object storage without any data movement or ingestion.
